docs.title: YSharp Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the documentation for YSharp.

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text: This directory contains all data and documentation related to performance benchmarks.

docs.title: Data
docs.text:
This folder stores JSON files with the data required to generate benchmark documentation. To reduce file size, only a subset of the BenchmarkDotNet output is saved.

These files are generated automatically whenever a benchmark completes.

docs.title: Plots
docs.text: This folder contains generated plot images used in the benchmark summaries. The plots visualize performance metrics over time and are automatically created and updated as new data becomes available.

docs.title: Summaries
docs.text:
This folder contains markdown summaries for individual benchmarks. Each summary includes:

  • The Git commit hash corresponding to the benchmarked code
  • The execution timestamp of the benchmark run
  • Plots showing performance trends over time

Summaries are automatically generated or updated whenever new benchmark results are recorded.

docs.title: How To Run
docs.text:
If you just run dotnet run in the benchmark directory it wont fully work as expectet.

Please use the provided .bat file if possible. If not possible you need to get your current (full) git commit hash and pass it as the first argument to the programm.

docs.title: Built-in Variables
docs.text:
• TRUE: Boolean value representing true.
  • FALSE: Boolean value representing false.
  • MATH: Provides mathematical functions and constants (see separate documentation for available math operations).

docs.title: PRINT(value)
docs.text: Prints the given value to the console. - Usage: PRINT("Hello World") - Arguments: value (any type) - Returns: null

docs.title: INPUT()
docs.text: Prompts the user for input and returns the entered string. - Usage: INPUT() - Arguments: none - Returns: string

docs.title: RUN(fileName)
docs.text: Executes a YSharp script from the specified file. - Usage: RUN("script.ys") - Arguments: fileName (string, path to script) - Returns: null (errors are printed to the console)

docs.title: TIMETORUN(fileName)
docs.text: Measures and returns the execution time of a script, broken down by interpreter phases. - Usage: TIMETORUN("script.ys") - Arguments: fileName (string, path to script) - Returns: string with timing information for each phase: - Init Lexer - Create Tokens - Init Parser - Create AST - Init Context - Run Interpreter - Whole Time (ms)

docs.title: TIME()
docs.text: Returns the current system time as a date-time value. - Usage: TIME() - Arguments: none - Returns: date-time object

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: A simple 2 state variable. Either TRUE or FALSE

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: VAR myVar = TRUE

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description - - -

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Parameters Returns Description - - - -

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Special behaviors, limitations, or performance considerations.
  • Anything unique compared to other types.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This datatype stores the date and the time. This can be usefull to Timestamp things.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: VAR myVar = TIME()

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description Microsecond Number Returns the Microseconds stored Millisecond Number Returns the Milliseconds stored Second Number Returns the Seconds stored Minute Number Returns the Minutes stored Hour Number Returns the Hours stored DayOfMonth Number Returns the Day of the Month stored DayOfWeek String Returns the Day of the Week stored DayOfYear Number Returns the Day of the year stored Month Number Returns the Month stored Year Number Returns the Year stored

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Parameters Returns Description - - - -

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: • You can currently only create a date time by using TIME()

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: A list can store multiple values (called elements). Elements can be Added, Removed or Searched for. You can accsses an element with the Get Function

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: VAR myVar = [1, 2, 3, 4]

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description Length Number Returns the length of the list

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Parameters Returns Description Add Any number of any type - Adds all the Elements to the List Get index: Number Any Returns the element at the specified index Remove index: Number - Removes the element at the specified positions IndexOf element: Any Number Returns the index of the element in the list if found. Else -1

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: • A List can countain multiple datatypes at once

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: A Number can store both Fractional and Whole Numbers.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
VAR myVar = 12
VAR pi = 3.1415

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description - - -

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Parameters Returns Description ToString - String Converts the Number to a string

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: A string stores a series of characters.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: VAR myVar = "Hello World"

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description Length Number Returns the Length of the string

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Parameters Returns Description ToNumber - Number Tryes to convert the string to a number. Throws if not succsessful ToBool - Bool Returns a bool based of if the string is not empty ToUpper - - Returns a new string with all characters changed to Uppercase ToLower - - Returns a new string with all characters changed to Lowercase Split splitString:String List Splits the string when encountering the splitString and returns a list containing one or multiple strings, depending on how many times splitString has ben found

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Short description of what this type represents and how it’s used.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: VAR myVar = <example_value>

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description <prop> <type> What it does

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Function Parameters Returns Description <func> <param>: <type> <type> What it does

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Special behaviors, limitations, or performance considerations.
  • Anything unique compared to other types.

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
In Ysharp, variables are immutable. This means once a variable is created, its value cannot be changed directly. If you want a variable to hold a new value, you must create a new variable using the VAR keyword.

For example:

VAR x = 5
VAR x = 10   // this creates a new variable x with value 10

Even though it looks like you’re reassigning x, you’re actually creating a new version of x each time.

docs.title: Variable Types
docs.text:
Currently, YSharp supports four main types of variables:

  1. String

  2. Text values wrapped in quotes.

  3. Example:

    YSharp VAR name = "Alice"

  4. Number

  5. Whole numbers or decimals.

  6. Example:

    YSharp VAR age = 25 VAR pi = 3.14

  7. Bool

  8. A 2 state variable

  9. Example:

    YSharp VAR isVegan = TRUE VAR containsNuts = FALSE

  10. DateTime

  11. Represents dates and times.

  12. Example:

    YSharp VAR now = TIME()

  13. List

  14. A collection of values, which can be of the same or mixed types.

  15. Example:

    YSharp VAR items = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"] VAR numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Variables in YSharp never change after being created.
  • To "update" a variable, you must create a new one with the same name.
  • There are currently four data types: String, Number, Bool, DateTime, and List.

docs.title: Creating a "Hello World" script
docs.text:
In this guide, you'll learn two simple ways to write and run your first "Hello World" program in Ysharp:

  • Directly in the interactive shell
  • From a saved script file

This is the first step to get comfortable with Ysharp.

docs.title: Option 1: Using the Shell (REPL)
docs.text: Running "Hello World" interactively is quick and easy:

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open the Ysharp shell by running the Ysharp.exe file:
  2. At the prompt, type:
>>> PRINT("Hello World")

You should see:

Hello World

Thats it! You've just executed your first line of Ysharp code.

docs.title: Option 2: Running from a Script File
docs.text: You can also write your code in a separate file and run it through the shell. This is useful for larger programs or saving your work.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
Open any text editor (Notepad, VS Code, etc.).

Create a new file and name it helloworld.ys or whatever you like. I recommend using the .ys extension for Ysharp scripts, but it's not required.

Then write the following code into the file:

PRINT("Hello World")

Save the file. Open the terminal and navigate to your YSharp executable. Then you will need to run the executable with a path argument like this:

.\YSharp.exe --path "path/to/your/file/helloworld.ys"

Replace "path/to/your/file/helloworld.ys" with the actual path to where you saved your file. The path can be absolute or relative.

You should see the output:

Hello World

docs.title: Installation of Ysharp
docs.text: Here you will learn how to install Ysharp. When you have completed this step you are ready to use the shell or to execute your own scripts.

docs.title: Prerequesites
docs.text:
Before installing Ysharp, make sure the following tools are installed on your system:

  • .NET SDK (>= 10) required to build and run Ysharp
  • Git to clone the repository

You can verify that they are installed using the following commands in a terminal:

dotnet --version
git --version

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repo
docs.text:
Use Git to clone the Ysharp source code from GitHub:

git clone https://github.com/xXrederXx/YSharp.git

This will create a new folder named YSharp in your current directory.

docs.title: 2. Navigate to the Project Folder
docs.text:
Change directory into the newly cloned repository:

cd ysharp

Now navigate into the source code folder named src

cd src

docs.title: 3. Build the Project
docs.text:
Use the .NET CLI to compile Ysharp in release mode:

dotnet build -c Release

If successful, the compiled executable will be located in:

./bin/Release/net9.0/

Look for a file named Ysharp.exe.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: You can now run the shell. A guid on how you can use the shell is here

docs.title: Guide on the Shell
docs.text: The Ysharp Shell is an interactive environment where you can write, test, and execute code line by line. This guide will show you how to use the shell effectively, from simple expressions to multi-line functions.

docs.title: What is the Shell?
docs.text:
When you launch the Ysharp.exe executable, it opens the Ysharp Shell. It reads your input and evaluates it.

This is useful for:

  • Quickly testing code
  • Debuggings
  • Learning the syntax
  • Running small scripts without saving files

Example shell prompt:

>>> PRINT("Hello World")

docs.title: Running Code
docs.text:
You can type simple one-line statements and press Enter. The shell doesn’t support traditional multi-line input, but you can write multi-line code using semicolons (\;) to separate statements on a single line.

If you want to run files from the shell, use the RUN command.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Running Hello World

>>> PRINT("Hello World")

Declaring a function and calling it

>>>FUN add(a, b);RETURN a+b;END;
>>>PRINT(add(10, 6))
16

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: You are now ready to write your Hello World script. A guid on how you can do this is here

docs.title: YSharp Keywords
docs.text:
Keywords are predefined, reserved identifiers that have special meanings to the interpreter. They can't be used as identifiers in your program. Using them could cause errors or unexpected behaviour.

Here is a table of all keywords currently supportet in YSharp. Clicking on them will take you to their dedicated article.

AND BREAK CATCH CONTINUE ELIF ELSE END FOR FUN IF IMPORT NOT OR RETURN STEP THEN TO TRY VAR WHILE

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Briefly explain what the keyword does and why it exists. Keep this high-level and intuitive. A beginner should understand the purpose after reading this section.

Use one or two short paragraphs. Avoid formal language where possible.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: <keyword> <syntax>

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
Describe when and why a developer would use this keyword.

  • Common scenarios where this keyword is helpful
  • What problem it solves
  • What it helps express more clearly or safely

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
// Minimal example demonstrating the keyword

Explain what happens in simple terms.

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
// A more realistic or idiomatic usage

Explain why this pattern is useful.

docs.title: Edge or Advanced Example (Optional)
docs.text:
// Less common but important usage

Explain what makes this case different or noteworthy.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Optional section for important clarifications:

  • Constraints or limitations
  • Common mistakes
  • Things that may surprise users

Keep this practical and concise.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• <related-keyword>
  • <another-keyword>
  • Concept: <related-concept>

docs.title: VAR
docs.text:
Declares a variable and assigns a value to it. YSharp variables are dynamically typed, so you don’t have to declare their type.

VAR x = 7
VAR name = "Alice"

You can not change the value later without redeclaring:

x = 10 # error
VAR x = 10 # right

docs.title: IF
docs.text:
Starts a conditional block. You must close it with END.

IF x == 5 THEN
    PRINT("x = 5")
END

docs.title: THEN
docs.text:
Used after a condition or loop definition to start its block of code.

IF x == 5 THEN
    PRINT("x = 5")
END

docs.title: ELIF
docs.text:
Short for “else if”. Checks another condition if the previous IF (or another ELIF) was false.

IF x == 5 THEN
    PRINT("x = 5")
ELIF x == 7 THEN
    PRINT("x = 7")
END

docs.title: ELSE
docs.text:
Runs if none of the IF or ELIF conditions are true.

IF x == 5 THEN
    PRINT("x = 5")
ELIF x == 7 THEN
    PRINT("x = 7")
ELSE
    PRINT("x is not 5 or 7")
END

docs.title: FOR
docs.text:
Creates a counted loop. You must use TO to set the ending value, and optionally STEP to set the increment. The end value is inclusive.

FOR i = 0 TO 7 THEN
    PRINT(i)
END

docs.title: TO
docs.text:
Used inside a FOR loop to set the loop’s end value.

FOR i = 0 TO 7 THEN
    PRINT(i)
END

docs.title: STEP
docs.text:
Changes how much the loop variable changes each time. Default is 1.

FOR i = 0 TO 7 STEP 0.1 THEN
    PRINT(i)
END

You can also step backwards:

FOR i = 10 TO 0 STEP -2 THEN
    PRINT(i)
END

docs.title: WHILE
docs.text:
Runs a block repeatedly while a condition is true. Can run forever if the condition never becomes false.

VAR x = 0
WHILE x < 10 THEN
    x++
END

docs.title: FUN
docs.text:
Defines a function. Arguments are separated by commas. Functions must end with END.

FUN add(a, b)
    RETURN a + b
END

docs.title: END
docs.text: Closes any block started by IF, FOR, WHILE, FUN, TRY, or CATCH. Always required — indentation does not matter.

docs.title: TRY
docs.text:
Starts a block of code where you want to catch errors.

TRY
    PRINT(undefinedVar)
CATCH err
    PRINT("Error caught: ", err)
END

Note: TRY/CATCH is not fully implemented yet.

docs.title: CATCH
docs.text: Runs if an error happens in the matching TRY block. Can take a variable to store the error.

docs.title: IMPORT
docs.text:
(Not yet implemented) Will be used to load code from another file.

IMPORT "path/to/file"

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
IMPORTANT this feature is not ready yet. using it could break your code or cause unexpected behaviour.

The Try-Catch keywords can be used to handle exceptions. when an exception occurs inside a try block, the code will continue in the catch where you can handle it. This is usefull to prevent crashing the application.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: // IN DEVELOPMENT

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: You would normaly use this if it could be possible that a function dosnt execute right and might throw. Then you can wrap the code in try catch and handle the exception.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text: // IN DEVELOPMENT

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text: // IN DEVELOPMENT

docs.title: Edge or Advanced Example (Optional)
docs.text: // IN DEVELOPMENT

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Do not use it yet. read the comment at the top of the page.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• <related-keyword>
  • <another-keyword>
  • Concept: <related-concept>

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The BREAK keyword is used to stop a loop imideately. It can only be used inside a loop. The code after the break wont be executed. It exists so you can easily quit a loop without needing to create a giant if around its contents.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: BREAK

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: You should use it when you want to stop iterating over the loop. This can be usefull if you are searching something in a loop and only want the first fit.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
This example will iterate over the values 0 to 5. But because there is an if check for the number 3 where it will break, it will only print from 0 to 2.

FOR i = 0 TO 5 THEN
    IF i == 3 THEN
        BREAK
    END
    PRINT(i)
END

>> 0
>> 1
>> 2

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
This example waits untill the user inputs yes.

WHILE TRUE THEN
    VAR inp = INPUT()
    IF inp == "yes" THEN
        BREAK
    END
END

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Be care full when working in nested loops. It can be hard to read and also cause unexpected behaviour.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• CONTINUE
  • RETURN

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The CONTINUE keyword is used to skip the rest a loop imideately. It can only be used inside a loop. The code after the continue wont be executed. It exists so you can easily skip code in a loop without needing to create a giant if around its contents.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: CONTINUE

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: You should use it when you want to skip an iteration in the loop. This can be usefull if you are first checking if an item is valid, and if it isnt you can just continue with the next items.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
This example will iterate over the values 0 to 5. But because there is an if check for the number 3 where it will continue, it will not print the number 3.

FOR i = 0 TO 5 THEN
    IF i == 3 THEN
        CONTINUE
    END
    PRINT(i)
END

>> 0
>> 1
>> 2
>> 4

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
This example will give you the numbers from 0 to 10 and ask you if you want to see then. If you dont want to see them it will continue. else it will show it to you and add it to the sum which gets printed at the end.

VAR sum = 0
FOR i = 0 TO 10 THEN
    PRINT("Want to use the number?")
    IF INPUT() == "no" THEN
        CONTINUE
    END
    PRINT(i)
    VAR sum = sum + i
END
PRINT(sum)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Be care full when working in nested loops. It can be hard to read and also cause unexpected behaviour.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BREAK
  • RETURN

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The RETURN keyword is used to exit a function imideately. It can only be used inside a function. The code on the next line after the return wont be executed. It exists so you can easily quit a function without needing to create a giant if around its contents.

Another capabilety of the return is to return a value out of a function. This is havely used.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
Return no value, just stop the function

RETURN

Return a value and stop the function

RETURN TRUE

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: You should use it when you want to stop a function and optionaly want to return a result. This is usefull in many ways and you will start using it as soon as you start working with functions.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
This example declares a method which returns when the for loop is on the number 3.

FUN foo()
    FOR i = 0 TO 5 THEN
        IF i == 3 THEN
            RETURN
        END
        PRINT(i)
    END
END
>> 0
>> 1
>> 2

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
Commonly its used when a function has finished generating a value. for example by doubeling its input.

FUN double(n)
    RETURN n * 2
END

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: A function dosn't need a return. Not every path needs to return the same value or even the same type.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• CONTINUE
  • BREAK

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The AND keyword is used as a logical operator. It takes two boolan values (one left and one right to it) and combines them to a single boolean. If both are true the result will also be true. Otherwise it will be false.

It exists to make more compact code. For example reducing the number of if checks.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: {boolen expression} AND {boolen expression}

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
A common use case is to check two values if both are true in a single if statement instead of using two nested ones. It also makes the code more readable.

An example could be if you want to check the weather. You have a boolen isSunny and another boolean isHot. Then you want to print if it is sunny and hot. Here would be the corresponding code.

IF isSunny AND isHot THEN
    PRINT("its sunny and hot")
END

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
VAR isSunny = TRUE
VAR isHot = TRUE

IF isSunny AND isHot THEN
    PRINT("its sunny and hot")
END

>> its sunny and hot

VAR isSunny = TRUE
VAR isHot = FALSE

IF isSunny AND isHot THEN
    PRINT("its sunny and hot")
END

>> "null"

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
VAR isSunny = TRUE
VAR tempCelsius = 35

IF isSunny AND tempCelsius > 30 THEN
    PRINT("its sunny and hot")
END

>> its sunny and hot

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: This does not perform a bitwise AND operation. It can only use single booleans.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• NOT
  • OR

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The NOT keyword is used as a logical operator. It takes one boola value (one right to it) and flips it. If the start value was true it outputs false. If the start value was false it outputs true.

It exists to make more compact code. For example by inverting an if call.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: NOT {boolen expression}

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
A common use case is to invert a fuction return value if it returns a boolean indicating a success. It also makes the code more readable.

An example could be if you have a function isGreaterThan(a, b) and this function returns a value and you want to print if the number is smaller or equal. In other words if the number is not greater than.

IF NOT isGreaterThan(2, 4) THEN
    PRINT("2 is not bigger than 4")
END

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
VAR isSunny = FALSE

IF NOT isSunny THEN
    PRINT("its not sunny")
END

>> its sunny and hot

VAR isHot = TRUE

IF NOT isHot THEN
    PRINT("its cold")
END

>> "null"

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
VAR input = "idk"

IF NOT (input == "yes" OR input == "no") THEN
    PRINT("invalide input")
END

>> invalide input

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This does not perform a bitwise NOT operation. It can only use single booleans.

You can theoreticaly chain NOTs but every second not will cancel it self.

VAR isHot = TRUE

IF isHot THEN
    ...
END

is same as

IF NOT NOT isHot THEN
    ...
END

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• AND
  • OR

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The OR keyword is used as a logical operator. It takes two boolan values (one left and one right to it) and combines them to a single boolean. If at least one of both is true the result will also be true. Otherwise it will be false.

It exists to make more compact code. For example reducing the number of if checks.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: {boolen expression} OR {boolen expression}

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
A common use case is to check two values if one or both values are true in a single if statement instead of using two nested ones. It also makes the code more readable.

An example could be if you want to check the weather. You have a boolen isRaining and another boolean isSnowing. Then you want to print if it is raining or snowing so you know to take a jacket hot. Here would be the corresponding code.

IF isRaining OR isSnowing THEN
    PRINT("take a jacket")
END

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
VAR isRaining = TRUE
VAR isSnowing = TRUE

IF isRaining OR isSnowing THEN
    PRINT("take a jacket")
END

>> take a jacket

VAR isRaining = TRUE
VAR isSnowing = FALSE

IF isRaining AND isSnowing THEN
    PRINT("take a jacket")
END

>> "null"

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
VAR input = "yes"

IF input == "yes" OR input == "no" THEN
    PRINT("valide input")
END

>> valide input

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: This does not perform a bitwise OR operation. It can only use single booleans.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• NOT
  • AND

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
In this file i will explain mostly the ELSE part of an IF-ELSE statement. To get information about the IF or ELIF part, go the the corresponding docs. You can find the links here

When an if condition turns out to be false and also all ELIF blocks are false the else code block will be executed. This is usefull for doing a something if everithing else is false.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
IF <condition> THEN
    <code>
END

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: You can use this after almost every IF check. For example by checking the user input to be any other value than to the ones checked.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
VAR inp = INPUT()
IF inp == "yes" THEN
    PRINT("inp was yes")
ELSE
    PRINT("inp was not yes")
END

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
It is even more usefull if you have multiple elifs. Just like here:

VAR tempCelsius = -20

IF tempCelsius > 30 THEN
    PRINT("hot")
ELIF tempCelsius > 15
    PRINT("Normal Temperature")
ELIF tempCelsius > 0
    PRINT("Slightly Cold")
ELSE
    PRINT("Its freezing")
END

>> Its freezing

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• IF
  • IF-ELIF

docs.title: Description
docs.text: If is used to make the code work with the values it gets. You give it a condition and if it is true the body of the if will be executed. This is a key concept of coding and can be used in many ways.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
IF <condition> THEN
    <code>
END

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: One use case could to check if the user inputed yes or no. You would just need to write the right condition. In this case it could look like the example in the next section.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
VAR inp = INPUT()
IF inp == "yes" THEN
    PRINT("inp was yes")
END

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
Combined with operators you can even write more complex if statements. For example by checking if it is sunny and hot.

VAR isSunny = TRUE
VAR tempCelsius = 35

IF isSunny AND tempCelsius > 30 THEN
    PRINT("its sunny and hot")
END

>> its sunny and hot

  • IF-ELIF

docs.title: Max Proccessable Input
docs.text:
You cant build massive projects in this language. There are several limitations on the Input. In short these are:

  • Max Chars per file: $2^{32} = 4.294.967.296$
  • Max Lines per file: $2^{14} = 16.384$
  • Max Columns per Line: $2^{10} = 1.024$
  • Max used files: $2^{8} = 256$

This is because the internaly used Position struct is restricted to store all these values in a ulong. These values are then packed as follows:

Index Line Column FileId 32 bits 14 bits 10 bits 8 bits
